name: Financial Ledger
description: SCOPE DECOMPOSITION. Tally is split into LEDGER (canonical accounts, transactions, spend categories), CLASSIFY (automatic suggestions and correction feedback), INGEST (PDF/CSV/Excel parsing and import review), BUDGET (monthly category amounts), INSIGHTS (daily/weekly/monthly analysis), and CHANNELS (optional web or messaging delivery). LEDGER is framed first because every downstream result depends on its financial facts.

ROOT PROBLEM AND JOURNEY. Dirk's spend is fragmented across three accounts at two banks. Budget and insight requests are symptoms: they cannot be trustworthy until account, transaction, transfer, and category facts have one precise, durable, agent-operable source. The 5 Whys chain is captured in the problem statement. Without a solution, consolidation remains delayed and manual, transfers or duplicates can distort spend, and category corrections cannot improve later analysis. The user journey is agent-first: discover a command and its schema, register an account, record or query a transaction, assign or correct a category, and receive a structured result that downstream modules can consume.

RECOMMENDED APPROACH A - LOCAL-FIRST CLI APPLICATION CORE. One self-contained Tally executable runs commands in-process against one embedded local datastore. The CLI is the stable public boundary: self-documenting help and schemas, JSON input/output, structured command results, deterministic exit codes, and version-matched skills. Reuse the intent of CORE vertical-slice, explicit-DI, and CommandResult prior art while deliberately rejecting Actions-specific HTTP endpoints, Aspire, PostgreSQL, RLS, multi-tenancy, and frontend assumptions. Web and WhatsApp are not selected now; an AI host or Hermes-like adapter invokes the CLI, and CHANNELS may later add a human surface. Complexity M; fits the budget; root-problem completeness 9/10; strongly builds on selected CORE conventions. Biggest risk: an under-specified embedded schema, migration, backup, or CLI compatibility policy could make a locally simple tool unreliable over years of financial history. Stress test: this approach fails if simultaneous channels, scheduled jobs, or remote access demonstrably require an always-on multi-client service; that evidence triggers a later ADR rather than speculative infrastructure now.

ALTERNATIVE B - SERVICE-FIRST SINGLE BINARY. The executable supports a persistent local service mode and the CLI is a thin client; web, messaging, and schedulers call the same service API. Complexity H; exceeds the initial one-process/no-daemon budget; root-problem completeness 10/10; builds less directly on the requested CLI-only operating model. Pros: natural concurrency and future channels. Cons: service lifecycle, local authentication, ports, upgrades, availability, and operational support before any ledger value exists. Biggest risk: infrastructure and security work dominate the finance domain. Rejected for the first release, but it remains an evolution path if Approach A's stress condition becomes true.

ALTERNATIVE C - DO LESS BY ADAPTING AN EXISTING FINANCE CLI. Store the ledger in an established personal-finance tool and build only Tally command aliases, normalized JSON projections, and agent skills. Complexity L; fits the budget; estimated root-problem completeness 6/10 until proven; maximally builds on existing software rather than CORE implementation patterns. Pros: mature ledger behavior and lower build cost. Cons: Tally's account/category/budget semantics, import provenance, stable agent schemas, and skill distribution may not align; private-database adapters would be unacceptable. Biggest risk: the adapter becomes a leaky compatibility layer that cannot guarantee Tally's financial or agent contract. This remains an honest discovery candidate and is elevated to a monitored kill criterion.

SELECTION. Choose Approach A because it solves the root problem with the smallest architecture that preserves a deliberate path to Approach B, while Approach C gets a fair discovery check before custom development proceeds. The non-obvious inversion is that Tally does not need to choose web versus WhatsApp: it needs a stable finance command boundary, and the surrounding agent ecosystem owns conversation.

SCOPE SIGNALS AND ROUTING. Signals present: sensitive financial-data security; external statement and possible banking boundaries; a product-wide model exceeding five entities; cross-system flows from bank files through agents to reports; future scheduled reporting; and significant unknowns around amount/date semantics, transfers, classification, encryption, and existing-tool fit. Security and significant unknowns are independently high-impact, and six signals are present. Route to /lex:discovery LEDGER before PRD. Project-shaping choices that later deserve /lex:adr are local embedded storage, channel-neutral CLI as the public boundary, and any transition to service mode.

DISCOVERY 0.2 — ACTORS AND CURRENT STATE. Human actor P1 Personal Finance Owner is the sole owner and decision-maker for household finances. AI Agent Host is a supporting automated client, not a persona. Today the owner obtains separate records from two banks, manually consolidates selected transactions, infers categories, removes suspected transfers or duplicates, and recalculates period totals. The pain is delayed work, inconsistent decisions, weak provenance, and no deterministic reconciliation proof.

DESIRED WORKFLOWS AND GAPS. UC-LEDGER-001 maintains owned accounts; UC-LEDGER-002 records precise canonical transactions; UC-LEDGER-003 assigns/corrects allocations; UC-LEDGER-004 confirms owned-account transfers; UC-LEDGER-005 returns snapshot-consistent reconcilable actuals; UC-LEDGER-006 lets a compatible agent discover and safely invoke the contract; UC-LEDGER-007 creates, verifies, and restores backups; UC-LEDGER-008 maintains the category catalogue; UC-LEDGER-009 voids/supersedes erroneous facts without erasure; UC-LEDGER-010 confirms full/partial refunds and their category offset; UC-LEDGER-011 revokes/replaces erroneous transfer/refund relationships attributably; UC-LEDGER-012 upgrades or migrates storage with a verified recovery point and atomic activation. Each workflow preserves the owner as primary actor while the AI Agent Host executes delegated public commands. File parsing, auto-classification, budget performance, UI, banking credentials and multi-user service operation remain in their brainstorm modules/non-goals.

PRE-MORTEM. Six months after launch, failure is most likely if (1) duplicate or semantically wrong transactions make confident totals untrustworthy — RISK-LEDGER-001 and 003; (2) local data or backups are lost, disclosed, or unrecoverable — RISK-LEDGER-002 and 007; or (3) CLI schemas and bundled skills drift so agents stop maintaining the ledger correctly — RISK-LEDGER-004. These drove the deep areas and the idempotency, integrity, recovery, and compatibility outcomes.
DOMAIN AREA DISPOSITIONS.
- auth/authz — CHECK: single-owner local scope uses the invoking OS identity as the trust boundary; A7 and NG6 require reassessment for any service/web/multi-user mode.

- data model and persistence — DEEP: exact facts, provenance, immutable source fields, void/supersession, category corrections, transfer/refund relationships, idempotency, atomicity, snapshot-consistent queries, archival, migration and reconciliation are central to G1-G4/G6-G8 and RISK-LEDGER-001/003/007.
- UI and navigation — N/A: NG4 assigns all screens and five-state UI behavior to CHANNELS; LEDGER has no frontend.

- integrations — DEEP: AI Agent Host is the only adopted client dependency; hledger was evaluated against the custom-development kill criterion and retained only as analogous/interoperability prior art.
- background and async — N/A: C1 forbids a required daemon and LEDGER has no scheduled/background workflow; future scheduled reporting belongs INSIGHTS/CHANNELS.

- reporting and analytics — CHECK: LEDGER owns exact filtered transaction membership and totals through UC-LEDGER-005; budget pace, narrative insight and daily/weekly/monthly presentation remain INSIGHTS.
- notifications — N/A: no Ledger goal emits notifications; delivery is a CHANNELS concern.
- configuration and admin — CHECK: the owner manages accounts and categories through the same public command contract; referenced records archive instead of losing historical meaning.
- multi-tenancy and isolation — N/A: NG6 fixes a single-user local Ledger; Actions RLS/tenancy prior art is explicitly rejected.
- financial semantics — DEEP: sign/date/currency, refunds, cash, transfers, category allocation, duplicate replay and exact totals directly determine whether the root problem is solved.
- local security and recovery — DEEP: sensitive history, backup copies, migration and at-rest posture drive C4/C11-C13 and the security kill criterion.

INTEGRATED DOMAIN RULES. Source account identity, original description, exact signed amount, currency, supplied bank dates, source reference and provenance are immutable canonical facts. Erroneous facts are voided or superseded through attributable history and default active actuals count only the valid outcome. Category, transfer and refund/reversal decisions are also attributable. Only active transactions and active relationships affect actuals; void/supersession atomically retires involved links, and reasoned revocation/replacement creates history rather than editing links or silently copying them to replacement transactions. Identical idempotent replay is a no-duplicate success; conflicting replay is a stable failure. A confirmed transfer requires distinct owned deposit accounts with same-currency equal/opposite principal amounts; dates may differ after explicit confirmation and embedded fees remain unlinked. A linked same-account/currency opposite-sign refund may be partial but cumulative refunds cannot exceed the original and follow its current allocation. Referenced accounts/categories archive rather than disappear; archived categories remain on history but cannot receive new assignments/corrections until reactivated. Paginated queries retain first-page membership/totals through a snapshot-bound cursor. Writes are atomic and integrity failures fail closed. Backups are consistent, version-identifiable, independently verifiable, and restored only after validation and explicit replacement authorization. Upgrades/migrations validate a candidate against counts, totals, relationships, integrity and version before atomic activation; failure leaves the source authoritative.

SECURITY AND COMPLIANCE. Selective threat analysis found disclosure, tampering, correction repudiation and data-loss/denial risks; owner-only artifact permissions, no secret/payload logging, immutable correction provenance, atomic writes and verified recovery address them. Spoofing/elevation are bounded by the single trusted OS identity and become new work if that boundary changes. POPIA Act 4 of 2013 section 6(1)(a) excludes purely personal or household activity, so no LEDGER regulatory constraint is added for the current sole-owner local scope; any hosted, multi-person, commercial or third-party processing in CHANNELS must reassess that inference.

FEASIBILITY. G1 known/high confidence via stable local identity patterns; G2 complex/high confidence provided exact numeric/date semantics are enforced; G3 known/medium-high pending owner validation of flat/single allocation assumptions; G4 complex/medium pending representative transfer/refund examples; G5 known/high using CORE structured-result discipline plus executable schemas; G6 complex/high with exact selected-set reconciliation; G7 known/high through idempotency contracts; G8 uncertain/low until OQ-LEDGER-8 spikes maintained embedded storage, permission, encryption, backup and key-recovery behavior. That low-confidence goal has both an explicit spike question and RISK-LEDGER-007.

EXISTING TOOL AND KILL-CRITERIA FINDING. hledger publicly demonstrates accounts, double-entry transfers, CSV rules, idempotent import state and JSON-capable reports, but a thin alias does not provide Tally's immutable source/correction provenance, separated category-allocation contract, uniform JSON input/result/error schema, version-matched agent skills, or the accepted verified-recovery outcome. Satisfying G2/G3/G5/G7/G8 would require a substantial semantic layer and adopt double-entry accounting contrary to NG7; the all-current-goals custom-development kill criterion is not triggered.

ASSUMPTION BASELINE FOR PRD. A1-A13 keep unresolved owner choices non-blocking: ZAR-only; deposit accounts; signed account delta; transaction date as Effective Date; one flat active category allocation; explicit refund/cash policy; single OS identity/writer; retained history; maintained platform encryption; flat archivable categories; equal/opposite confirmed transfer principal; sufficiency of masked account identity fields; and rejection of new assignments/corrections to archived categories until reactivation. OQ-LEDGER-1 through 6 validate these defaults with real accounts/statements. OQ-LEDGER-8 is a pre-planning spike. OQ-LEDGER-7/9/10/12 are resolved and OQ-LEDGER-11 is deferred to CHANNELS.

SCOPE NOTE. Discovery added G7 idempotent retry safety and G8 verified recovery because they operationalize existing G2/G5 and C4/RISK-LEDGER-001/002/004; they do not add banking integration, UI, budgets, classification, imports, multi-user service behavior, or full accounting.

DISCOVERY REVIEW REMEDIATION. Fresh graph-only review added explicit category-catalogue maintenance, masked account-identity sufficiency, equal/opposite same-currency transfer principal rules, partial refund bounds, snapshot-consistent pagination, and void/supersession of erroneous transaction facts. It also updated the existing-tool kill criterion from the original six goals to every current goal G1-G8.

SECOND DISCOVERY REVIEW REMEDIATION. The final lifecycle pass made transaction and relationship correction compose safely: void/supersession retires active links atomically; explicit revoke/relink owns erroneous relationship correction; archived categories cannot receive new corrections; and G8 now carries analogous evidence plus UC-LEDGER-012 for verified atomic storage evolution.
problem_statement: Dirk cannot reliably understand personal spend across three bank accounts at two banks because transaction facts live in separate statements and descriptions and categories are inconsistent. 5 Whys: a transaction CLI is wanted because budgets and insights need actual spend; actual spend is unreliable because it is fragmented across accounts; manual aggregation is unreliable because normalization and category corrections are not durable; those corrections are not reusable because no canonical machine-operable record exists; root cause: Tally lacks an agent-operable local ledger that preserves precise account, transaction, and spend-category facts with provenance. Journey: Dirk or an AI agent registers an account, records or queries a transaction through discoverable JSON commands, assigns or corrects its spend category, and downstream modules consume the same canonical facts.
why_now: AI agents can now maintain a structured local ledger through a self-documenting CLI, replacing delayed manual consolidation before more statement history and budget decisions accumulate.
